did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper proposes a fast fully automatic skin lesions segmentation method using probabilistic characteristics with the Parzen window (SPPW). This computational modeling technique is applied to dermoscopic images from the PH2 and ISIC datasets to segment skin lesions, which is a key step in computer-assisted diagnosis of melanoma.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper proposes a novel method (SPPW) for fast fully automatic skin lesions segmentation, presents original experimental results using the PH2 and ISIC datasets, and reports specific performance metrics (e.g., Specificity 98.55%, Accuracy 95.48%). It is an original technical study rather than a review of prior literature.

title: Fast fully automatic skin lesions segmentation probabilistic with Parzen window
brief_summary: This study proposes a fast, fully automatic skin lesion segmentation method called SPPW, which utilizes probabilistic characteristics with the Parzen window. The approach was evaluated using dermoscopic images from the PH2 and ISIC datasets to aid in the diagnosis of cutaneous melanoma. The method achieved high performance metrics, including a specificity of 98.55%, accuracy of 95.48%, and Dice coefficient of 91.12% across the datasets. Key highlights of the SPPW method include its short average segmentation time per image and superior metric values compared to other existing methods. Consequently, the authors conclude that SPPW is a quick, viable, and accessible option for assisting in the diagnosis of diseased skin.

lesion_types_studied.details: The paper focuses on the segmentation of skin lesions, specifically mentioning that cutaneous melanoma accounts for over 90% of all melanoma cases. The method was evaluated on the PH2 and ISIC datasets to aid in the diagnosis of diseased skin, with melanoma being the primary clinical context mentioned.

Mentioned in the introduction as a category of skin cancer alongside melanoma, but the study's experiments and segmentation targets focus on lesions generally (from PH2/ISIC) with melanoma as the key diagnostic context.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion boundaries/masks in dermoscopic images
all_task_types.details: The paper proposes a fast fully automatic skin lesions segmentation method (SPPW) using probabilistic characteristics with the Parzen window. The model predicts pixel-wise masks for skin lesions to aid in the diagnosis of cutaneous melanoma. Performance is evaluated using segmentation metrics such as Dice coefficient (91.12%), Jaccard Index (84.90%), Specificity (98.55%), and Accuracy (95.48%).

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: SPPW (Fast fully automatic skin lesions segmentation probabilistic with Parzen window)
conclusions: The proposed SPPW method achieves high performance metrics, with average values across the PH2 and ISIC datasets of 98.55% Specificity, 95.48% Accuracy, 91.12% Dice coefficient, 88.45% Sensitivity, 87.86% Matthews correlation coefficient, and 84.90% Jaccard Index.
The SPPW method demonstrates a short average segmentation time per image, balancing efficiency and speed.
The metric values obtained by the SPPW method are often higher than those obtained by other existing methods.
The SPPW method is concluded to be a quick, viable, and easily accessible option to aid in the diagnosis of diseased skin.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on two public datasets (PH2 and ISIC) for evaluation, with no mention of external validation on independent clinical cohorts, limiting generalizability assessment.
The paper does not report specific image counts, training/testing/validation splits, or detailed dataset statistics for PH2 and ISIC, making reproducibility of the experimental setup difficult.

Performance metrics (Specificity 98.55%, Accuracy 95.48%, Dice 91.12%) are reported as averages across both datasets without providing per-dataset breakdowns, which may mask performance disparities between the datasets.

The method (SPPW) is a probabilistic segmentation approach using Parzen windows rather than a deep learning model; while fast, its reliance on handcrafted probabilistic features may limit its ability to capture complex lesion characteristics compared to modern deep learning architectures.

The paper claims the method is 'easily accessible' but does not provide code, pre-trained models, or detailed hyperparameter settings, hindering immediate clinical deployment or independent verification.

No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) is reported to support the claim that SPPW metrics are 'often higher' than other methods, relying instead on qualitative comparisons.
